name: Protesilaus
description: Protesilaus, son of Iphiclus and Astyoche, was a Thessalian hero in Greek mythology, known as the first Greek to die in the Trojan War. He hailed from Phylace and led a contingent of forty ships to Troy. Eager to prove his valor and heed the call of duty, he bravely leaped ashore as the first Greek soldier during the initial landing on Trojan soil. A prophecy foretold that the first Greek to set foot on Trojan land would be the first to die, but Protesilaus, driven by courage or perhaps unaware of the prophecy, disregarded the omen. He was immediately slain by Hector, or in some accounts, by Aeneas, Acamas, or Cycnus. His swift demise made him a symbol of sacrifice and patriotic duty. The gods, moved by the grief of his wife, Laodamia, allowed him to return from the underworld for a brief period to be with her. After his second death, Laodamia commissioned a life-sized bronze statue of him and spent her days embracing it, eventually taking her own life to join him in the afterlife. Protesilaus was venerated as a hero, with a sanctuary and games established in his honor at Elaius in the Thracian Chersonese, across from Troy. His story highlights themes of fate, courage, love, and the heavy cost of war. He represents the tragic hero who, despite his brief life, left an indelible mark on the Trojan War narrative and the hearts of those who mourned him. His sacrifice paved the way for the Achaean army to establish a beachhead, allowing them to continue their siege of Troy.
subtitle: First to fall at Troy.
achievements: First Greek to land at Troy

keyMyths.title: First to Land
keyMyths.description: Protesilaus, ignoring or unaware of the prophecy, was the first Greek to jump ashore at Troy. He was immediately killed, fulfilling the prophecy.

keyMyths.title: Laodamia's Grief
keyMyths.description: His wife, Laodamia, was overcome with grief and was granted a temporary reunion with Protesilaus by the gods. Unable to bear his second death, she committed suicide.

keyMyths.title: Heroic Cult
keyMyths.description: After his death, Protesilaus was worshipped as a hero, with a sanctuary built in his honor at Elaius. Games were also held in his memory.

sources.author: Homer
